---
title: "TechTrek Documentation"
subtitle: "Lecture & Event Booking Platform"
date: today
format:
  docx:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    documentclass: report
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    highlight-style: github
---

{{< pagebreak >}}

# At a Glance

> A full-stack lecture and event booking platform for tech conferences, built with FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, and Jinja2.

TechTrek enables universities and conference organizers to publish lecture sessions, manage auditorium seating layouts, and let students book seats with a real-time hold-and-pay flow. Attendees receive QR-coded tickets that supervisors scan at the door for check-in. An admin panel provides complete control over cities, colleges, auditoriums, speakers, sessions, bookings, waitlists, and user roles.

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Lecture/event booking platform for tech conferences and campus events |
| **Users** | Students (attendees), Supervisors (check-in staff), Admins (organizers) |
| **Core Flow** | Browse sessions → Select seats → Hold → Pay → Receive QR ticket |
| **Admin Capabilities** | Manage cities, colleges, auditoriums, speakers, sessions, bookings, waitlists, users |
| **Deployment** | Single Python process (uvicorn), PostgreSQL database, optional SMTP |

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Tech Stack

TechTrek follows a traditional server-rendered architecture rather than a modern SPA approach. The backend handles all routing, rendering, and business logic in a single Python process, keeping the deployment footprint small while still providing a rich, interactive user experience. The table below summarizes the technology choices at each layer and why they were selected.

| Layer | Technology | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Web Framework** | FastAPI | Async HTTP server, routing, middleware, dependency injection |
| **ORM** | SQLAlchemy | Database models, relationships, queries |
| **Templating** | Jinja2 | Server-side HTML rendering |
| **Database** | PostgreSQL | Primary data store (via `psycopg2-binary`) |
| **Auth** | bcrypt + Starlette Sessions | Password hashing and cookie-based session management |
| **QR Codes** | `qrcode[pil]` | Ticket QR generation for booking confirmations |
| **QR Scanner** | html5-qrcode (CDN) | Browser-based camera QR scanning for check-in |
| **Config** | pydantic-settings + `.env` | Type-safe configuration with environment variable overrides |
| **Email** | smtplib | Signup and booking confirmation emails |
| **Frontend** | Vanilla HTML/CSS/JS | No SPA framework; server-rendered with custom interactive components |

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Architecture Diagrams

The following diagrams illustrate how TechTrek is structured at different levels of abstraction -- from the high-level component layout, through the per-request processing pipeline, down to the data model and template inheritance tree. Together they give a complete picture of how a user's action in the browser flows through the system and back.

## System Architecture

The application follows a layered client-server-data pattern. The browser communicates with the FastAPI server over HTTP, where requests pass through session middleware before reaching route handlers. Routers delegate business logic to service modules that interact with PostgreSQL, and then render Jinja2 templates back to the browser. An optional SMTP connection handles outbound email notifications.

```{mermaid}
graph TB
    Browser --> Middleware
    Middleware --> Routers
    Routers --> Services
    Routers --> Templates
    Services --> DB[(PostgreSQL)]
    Services --> SMTP[SMTP]
    Templates --> Browser
    Browser --> Static[Static Files]
```

## Request Lifecycle

Every HTTP request passes through a well-defined pipeline. Starlette's session middleware loads or creates a session cookie first, then the router resolves FastAPI dependencies such as the database session and the current user. The router calls the appropriate service function for business logic, which queries or mutates the database, and finally the router renders a Jinja2 template with the result and returns the finished HTML page to the browser.

```{mermaid}
sequenceDiagram
    participant B as Browser
    participant R as Router
    participant S as Service
    participant DB as Database

    B->>R: HTTP Request
    R->>R: Auth + Dependencies
    R->>S: Business logic
    S->>DB: Query / Mutate
    DB-->>S: Result
    S-->>R: Response data
    R-->>B: Rendered HTML
```

## User Flow

The attendee journey begins with browsing published sessions and viewing details such as speaker info, agenda, and pricing. After registering or logging in, the user selects seats on an interactive map and the system holds them for five minutes while the user completes checkout. Upon payment, the system generates a unique ticket with a QR code for venue check-in. If a session is sold out, users can join a waitlist and receive priority access when an administrator opens additional capacity.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
    Browse[Browse] --> Detail[View Session]
    Detail --> Login[Register / Login]
    Login --> Seats[Select Seats]
    Seats --> Hold[Hold 5 min]
    Hold --> Pay[Checkout & Pay]
    Pay --> Ticket[QR Ticket]
    Ticket --> CheckIn[Scan at Venue]
    Detail -->|Sold out| Wait[Waitlist]
    Wait -->|Priority| Seats
```

## Booking State Machine

Each booking record progresses through a strict set of states. When a user selects seats, the booking enters the "Hold" state with a five-minute expiry timer. If payment is confirmed in time, it moves to "Paid" and a ticket is issued. From the paid state, it can either be scanned at the venue (checked in) or cancelled for a partial refund. If the hold timer expires before payment, the booking is automatically marked as expired and the seats are released.

```{mermaid}
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Hold
    Hold --> Paid: Payment
    Hold --> Expired: Timeout
    Paid --> Refunded: Cancel
    Paid --> CheckedIn: QR Scan
    Expired --> [*]
    Refunded --> [*]
    CheckedIn --> [*]
```

## Database Entity Relationships

The data model comprises 12 SQLAlchemy models organized into three logical domains. The **core booking flow** captures the central relationship chain: users book seats for lecture sessions hosted in auditoriums. The **location hierarchy** models the geographic nesting of cities, colleges, auditoriums, and seats. The **supporting models** cover speakers, agenda items, waitlists, testimonials, and newsletter subscribers. Full column-level details for every table are provided in the Database Schema section later in this document.

### Core Booking Flow

Users book seats for lecture sessions. Each booking links a user, a session, and a seat.

```{mermaid}
erDiagram
    User ||--o{ Booking : books
    LectureSession ||--o{ Booking : has
    Booking }o--|| Seat : reserves
    Auditorium ||--o{ Seat : contains
    Auditorium ||--o{ LectureSession : hosts
```

### Location Hierarchy

Cities contain colleges, which contain auditoriums.

```{mermaid}
erDiagram
    City ||--o{ College : contains
    College ||--o{ Auditorium : contains
    Auditorium ||--o{ Seat : contains
```

### Supporting Models

Speakers, agenda items, waitlists, testimonials, and newsletter subscribers.

```{mermaid}
erDiagram
    Speaker ||--o{ LectureSession : presents
    LectureSession ||--o{ AgendaItem : has
    LectureSession ||--o{ Waitlist : has
    User ||--o{ Waitlist : joins
```

## Template Hierarchy

TechTrek uses Jinja2 template inheritance to share common layout elements across all 33 HTML files. The root `base.html` template provides the navbar, footer, theme toggle, flash message display, starfield background, and shared JavaScript utilities. All public-facing, authentication, booking, error, and supervisor templates extend `base.html` directly. Admin pages add a second layer: `admin/base_admin.html` extends the base and inserts a sidebar navigation, and all admin-specific templates extend this intermediate layout instead.

### Public, Auth, Booking, and Supervisor Templates

```{mermaid}
graph TB
    Base[base.html]
    Base --> Public[5 public pages]
    Base --> Auth[login + register]
    Base --> Booking[5 booking pages]
    Base --> Errors[404 + 500]
    Base --> Supervisor[checkin.html]
```

### Admin Panel Templates

```{mermaid}
graph TB
    Base[base.html] --> Admin[base_admin.html]
    Admin --> Dashboard[dashboard]
    Admin --> CRUD[CRUD pages x12]
    Admin --> Checkin[checkin]
    Admin --> Waitlist[waitlist]
```

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Features Overview

TechTrek serves four distinct user roles, each with progressively broader capabilities. **Public visitors** can browse sessions and view ticket details without an account. **Registered users** gain access to the full booking flow -- selecting seats, paying, and managing their tickets. **Supervisors** are staff members who handle check-in at event venues by scanning QR codes. **Administrators** have complete control over the platform's content, users, and operations through a dedicated admin panel. The tables below detail the features available to each role.

## Public Visitors (No Login Required)

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Browse Sessions** | View all published upcoming lecture sessions with search and filter by city |
| **Session Details** | See speaker info, agenda, venue, pricing tiers, and real-time seat availability |
| **Availability Badges** | Sessions display "Available", "Filling Up", "Sold Out", or "Priority Only" badges |
| **View Tickets** | Anyone with a ticket link or group link can view ticket details and QR codes |
| **Newsletter** | Subscribe to email updates from the home page |
| **Light/Dark Theme** | Toggle between a dark cyberpunk theme and a light Frutiger Aero theme |

## Registered Users

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Account Registration** | Sign up with email, username, password, and optional profile fields (college, discipline, domain, year) |
| **Interactive Seat Selection** | Visual seat map with real-time availability; supports standard, VIP, and accessible seat types |
| **Multi-Seat Booking** | Select multiple seats in one transaction; generates a group ticket with a shared QR code |
| **5-Minute Hold** | Selected seats are held for 5 minutes while the user completes checkout |
| **Tiered Pricing** | Different prices for standard, VIP, and accessible seats |
| **QR-Coded Tickets** | Each booking generates a unique ticket ID and QR code for venue check-in |
| **My Bookings** | Dashboard showing active bookings and archived (cancelled/refunded) history |
| **Booking Cancellation** | Cancel paid bookings with automatic refund calculation (amount minus a fixed cancellation fee of 100) |
| **Waitlist** | Join a waitlist for sold-out sessions; receive priority booking access when new sessions are added |

## Supervisors

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **QR Check-In** | Scan attendee QR codes using the device camera to verify and mark check-in |
| **Manual Entry** | Enter a ticket ID manually when QR scanning is not possible |
| **Verification Feedback** | Immediate visual confirmation of valid/invalid/already-checked-in tickets |

## Administrators

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Dashboard** | Overview statistics: total users, active sessions, bookings, revenue |
| **City Management** | Create, edit, toggle active, and delete cities |
| **College Management** | Create, edit, and delete colleges linked to cities |
| **Auditorium Management** | Create and edit auditoriums with configurable row/column counts |
| **Seat Layout Designer** | Visual drag-and-paint tool for designing auditorium layouts with standard, VIP, accessible, and aisle seats; row/column gaps; stage width |
| **Speaker Management** | Create and edit speaker profiles with name, title, bio, photo, and email |
| **Session Management** | Create and edit lecture sessions with speaker, auditorium, schedule, pricing tiers, description, and banner; publish or keep as draft |
| **Agenda Builder** | Add ordered agenda items to sessions with speaker names and durations |
| **Booking Management** | View and filter all bookings; cancel or refund individual bookings |
| **CSV Export** | Export filtered booking data as CSV for reporting |
| **Check-In** | QR scanning and manual ticket verification (same as supervisor) |
| **Waitlist Management** | View waitlisted users; grant priority access to a target session for all waitlisted users of a source session |
| **User Management** | View all registered users; toggle admin and supervisor roles |

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Backend Architecture

The backend is built as a single FastAPI application using SQLAlchemy for database access and Jinja2 for server-side HTML rendering. Rather than exposing a JSON API consumed by a frontend SPA, TechTrek renders complete HTML pages on the server and returns them directly. This keeps the architecture simple -- a single Python process handles everything from authentication to template rendering -- while still supporting interactive features through targeted client-side JavaScript. The codebase is organized into models, routers, services, templates, and static assets, as outlined below.

## Project Structure

```
techtrak/
├── app/
│   ├── main.py                  # Application entry point, middleware, router registration
│   ├── config.py                # Pydantic-settings configuration
│   ├── database.py              # SQLAlchemy engine and session factory
│   ├── dependencies.py          # FastAPI dependencies (auth, templates, flash messages)
│   ├── models/
│   │   ├── __init__.py          # Re-exports all models
│   │   ├── user.py              # User model
│   │   ├── city.py              # City model
│   │   ├── college.py           # College model
│   │   ├── auditorium.py        # Auditorium model
│   │   ├── seat.py              # Seat model
│   │   ├── speaker.py           # Speaker model
│   │   ├── session.py           # LectureSession model
│   │   ├── agenda.py            # AgendaItem model
│   │   ├── booking.py           # Booking model
│   │   ├── waitlist.py          # Waitlist model
│   │   └── testimonial.py       # Testimonial and NewsletterSubscriber models
│   ├── routers/
│   │   ├── auth.py              # Authentication routes (/auth)
│   │   ├── public.py            # Public pages (/, /sessions, /ticket)
│   │   ├── booking.py           # Booking flow (/booking)
│   │   ├── admin.py             # Admin panel (/admin)
│   │   └── supervisor.py        # Supervisor check-in (/supervisor)
│   ├── services/
│   │   ├── booking.py           # Booking business logic
│   │   └── email.py             # Email sending service
│   ├── schemas/                 # (Reserved for Pydantic schemas)
│   ├── templates/               # Jinja2 HTML templates (33 files)
│   └── static/
│       ├── css/style.css        # Single stylesheet (~2,600 lines)
│       └── js/
│           ├── seat-picker.js   # Interactive seat selection
│           └── seat-designer.js # Admin seat layout editor
├── seed.py                      # Database seeding script
├── requirements.txt             # Python dependencies
├── .env                         # Environment overrides (not committed)
└── README.md                    # Quick-start guide
```

## Entry Point (`app/main.py`)

The application is created and configured in a single module:

1. **FastAPI instance** is created with the title `"TechTrek"`. API docs are available at `/api/docs` when `debug=True`.
2. **SessionMiddleware** is attached for cookie-based session management using the configured `secret_key`.
3. **Static files** are mounted at `/static` from `app/static/`.
4. **All models** are imported so SQLAlchemy registers them, then `Base.metadata.create_all()` creates tables.
5. **Schema migrations** run as `ALTER TABLE` statements inside a `try/except` block to add columns that were introduced after the initial schema (`price_vip`, `price_accessible`, `booking_group`, `group_qr_data`, `is_supervisor`).
6. **Routers** are included in order: auth, public, booking, admin, supervisor.
7. **Exception handlers** are registered for `AuthRedirect` (303 redirect to login), 404 (custom template), and 500 (custom template).

## Configuration (`app/config.py`)

Configuration uses pydantic-settings with automatic `.env` file loading.

| Setting | Type | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `database_url` | `str` | `postgresql+psycopg2://postgres:root@localhost:5432/techtrek` | Database connection string |
| `secret_key` | `str` | `techtrek-dev-secret-change-in-production` | Session cookie signing key |
| `debug` | `bool` | `True` | Enables `/api/docs` endpoint |
| `hold_timeout_minutes` | `int` | `5` | Minutes a seat hold remains valid |
| `priority_window_hours` | `int` | `24` | Hours a waitlist priority grant lasts |
| `smtp_host` | `str` | `""` | SMTP server hostname (empty = emails skipped) |
| `smtp_port` | `int` | `587` | SMTP server port |
| `smtp_user` | `str` | `""` | SMTP authentication username |
| `smtp_password` | `str` | `""` | SMTP authentication password |
| `smtp_from_email` | `str` | `noreply@techtrek.in` | Sender email address |
| `smtp_from_name` | `str` | `TechTrek` | Sender display name |

## Database Layer (`app/database.py`)

Minimal setup using SQLAlchemy's synchronous API:

- `engine` is created from `settings.database_url` with echo disabled.
- `SessionLocal` is a `sessionmaker` bound to the engine, yielding standard ORM sessions.
- `Base` is a `DeclarativeBase` subclass that all models inherit from.

## Dependencies (`app/dependencies.py`)

FastAPI dependency functions provide shared functionality to all route handlers:

| Dependency | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `get_db()` | Generator yielding `Session` | Creates a database session per request and closes it after |
| `get_current_user()` | `(Request, Session) -> Optional[User]` | Reads `user_id` from the session cookie and returns the `User` or `None` |
| `require_auth()` | `(Request, Session) -> User` | Same as above but raises `AuthRedirect` if no user is logged in |
| `require_admin()` | `(Request, Session) -> User` | Requires the user to be logged in AND have `is_admin=True` |
| `flash()` | `(Request, str, str)` | Stores a flash message in the session for display on next page load |
| `get_flashes()` | `(Request) -> list` | Pops and returns all pending flash messages |
| `template_ctx()` | `(Request, **kwargs) -> dict` | Builds the standard template context (`request`, `user`, `flashes`, plus any extras) |

`AuthRedirect` is a custom exception that carries a redirect URL. When raised, the global exception handler returns a 303 redirect to the login page with a `next` query parameter preserving the intended destination.

## Services

### Booking Service (`app/services/booking.py`)

Contains the core booking business logic:

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `get_seat_map(db, session_id, auditorium_id)` | Returns a list of seat dicts with `id`, `row`, `col`, `label`, `type`, and `status` (available/taken/aisle). A seat is "taken" if it has a paid booking or an active hold. |
| `hold_seats(db, user_id, session_id, seat_ids)` | Cancels any existing holds for the user on this session, then creates new `Booking` records with `payment_status="hold"` and `held_until` set to now + 5 minutes. Skips seats that are already taken. |
| `confirm_payment(db, user_id, session_id)` | Converts valid holds to paid bookings. Generates `ticket_id` (format: `TT-YYYYMMDD-XXXXXXXX`), QR code (base64 PNG), sets `amount_paid` based on seat type pricing, assigns `booking_group` and `group_qr_data` for multi-seat bookings, and sends confirmation emails. |
| `cancel_booking_user(db, booking_id, user_id)` | Cancels a paid booking. Applies a fixed cancellation fee of 100 and sets `payment_status="refunded"`. Returns the refund amount. |
| `cancel_existing_holds(db, user_id, session_id)` | Marks all hold-status bookings for a user/session as cancelled. |
| `get_user_bookings(db, user_id)` | Returns all paid and refunded bookings for a user, ordered by date descending. |

### Email Service (`app/services/email.py`)

Handles transactional emails via SMTP:

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `send_signup_confirmation(email, username)` | Sends a welcome email after registration |
| `send_booking_confirmation(email, username, session_title, seat_label, ticket_id, booking_ref)` | Sends a booking confirmation with seat details, ticket ID, and cancellation policy |

If `smtp_host` is empty (the default), emails are logged but not sent, allowing development without an SMTP server.

## Authentication System

TechTrek uses **session-based authentication** with **bcrypt password hashing**:

1. **Registration**: User submits email, username, password, and optional profile fields. The password is hashed with `bcrypt.hashpw()` and stored. The first registered user is automatically granted admin privileges (`is_admin=True`). A signup confirmation email is sent.

2. **Login**: User submits username/email and password. The system looks up the user by username or email, then verifies with `bcrypt.checkpw()`. On success, `user_id` is stored in the session cookie.

3. **Session Management**: Starlette's `SessionMiddleware` handles cookie-based sessions. The session is signed with `secret_key` to prevent tampering.

4. **Authorization Levels**:
   - **Public**: No authentication required
   - **User**: `require_auth()` -- must be logged in
   - **Supervisor or Admin**: Checked via `user.is_supervisor or user.is_admin`
   - **Admin only**: `require_admin()` -- must have `is_admin=True`

5. **Logout**: Clears the entire session via `request.session.clear()`.

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Database Schema

The database consists of 12 SQLAlchemy models that map to PostgreSQL tables. All models inherit from a shared `DeclarativeBase`, and tables are created automatically on application startup via `Base.metadata.create_all()`. The project does not use Alembic migrations; columns that were added after the initial schema (such as `price_vip`, `booking_group`, and `is_supervisor`) are applied through raw `ALTER TABLE` statements wrapped in `try/except` blocks inside `app/main.py`. This section documents each table, with full column-level detail for the four core entities and concise summaries for the simpler supporting tables.

## `users`

The `users` table stores account credentials, profile information, and role flags. Each user is identified by a unique email and username, and passwords are stored as bcrypt hashes. Two boolean flags -- `is_admin` and `is_supervisor` -- control authorization levels throughout the application. The first user to register is automatically promoted to admin. Optional profile fields capture the user's college, discipline, tech domain, and year of study.

| Column | Type | Constraints | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `id` | `Integer` | PK, indexed | Auto-increment primary key |
| `email` | `String(255)` | Unique, not null, indexed | Login email address |
| `username` | `String(100)` | Unique, not null, indexed | Login username |
| `password_hash` | `String(255)` | Not null | bcrypt password hash |
| `full_name` | `String(200)` | Nullable | Display name |
| `college` | `String(300)` | Nullable | College affiliation (free text) |
| `discipline` | `String(100)` | Nullable | Academic discipline |
| `domain` | `String(100)` | Nullable | Tech domain of interest |
| `year_of_study` | `Integer` | Nullable | Current year of study |
| `is_admin` | `Boolean` | Default `False` | Admin role flag |
| `is_supervisor` | `Boolean` | Default `False` | Supervisor role flag |
| `created_at` | `DateTime` | Default `utcnow` | Account creation timestamp |

**Relationships**: `bookings` (1:N Booking), `waitlist_entries` (1:N Waitlist)

## Location Tables: `cities`, `colleges`, `auditoriums`, `seats`

These four tables form a geographic hierarchy. **Cities** store a name, state, and an `is_active` flag for soft-deletion; each city has many colleges. **Colleges** belong to a city and record a name, address, and active status; each college has many auditoriums. **Auditoriums** are the physical venues where sessions take place. Beyond basic identity fields, they store the seating grid dimensions (`total_rows`, `total_cols`), optional stage width, and JSON-encoded row/column gap indices that control the visual layout of the seat map. Deleting a college cascades to its auditoriums, and deleting an auditorium cascades to its seats.

**Seats** represent individual positions within an auditorium grid. Each seat records its row and column number, a human-readable label (e.g., "A1", "B12"), a type (`standard`, `vip`, `accessible`, or `aisle`), and an active flag. Aisle-type seats act as visual spacers and cannot be booked.

## `speakers`

Speaker profiles store a name, professional title, biography, photo URL, and contact email. Each speaker can be linked to multiple lecture sessions. The speaker model is intentionally simple -- it provides display information for session detail pages rather than full user accounts.

## `lecture_sessions`

The `lecture_sessions` table is the central scheduling entity. Each session is linked to an auditorium (venue) and optionally to a speaker profile, and stores a title, description, banner image URL, start time, duration, and per-seat-type pricing. Three price columns support tiered pricing: `price` for standard seats, `price_vip` for VIP, and `price_accessible` for accessible seats. A `status` field controls visibility: sessions start as `draft`, are set to `published` when ready for public browsing, and can be marked `completed` afterward.

| Column | Type | Constraints | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `id` | `Integer` | PK, indexed | Auto-increment primary key |
| `auditorium_id` | `Integer` | FK -> `auditoriums.id`, not null | Venue |
| `speaker_id` | `Integer` | FK -> `speakers.id`, nullable | Linked speaker profile |
| `title` | `String(300)` | Not null | Session title |
| `speaker` | `String(200)` | Not null | Speaker name (display text) |
| `description` | `Text` | Nullable | Session description |
| `banner_url` | `String(500)` | Nullable | Banner image URL |
| `start_time` | `DateTime` | Not null | Scheduled start time |
| `duration_minutes` | `Integer` | Default `30` | Session duration |
| `price` | `Numeric(10,2)` | Not null, default `0` | Standard seat price |
| `price_vip` | `Numeric(10,2)` | Nullable | VIP seat price |
| `price_accessible` | `Numeric(10,2)` | Nullable | Accessible seat price |
| `status` | `String(20)` | Default `"draft"` | One of: `draft`, `published`, `completed` |
| `created_at` | `DateTime` | Default `utcnow` | Creation timestamp |

**Relationships**: `auditorium`, `speaker_rel`, `bookings` (1:N), `agenda_items` (1:N, cascade delete, ordered), `waitlist_entries` (1:N)

## `agenda_items`

Each lecture session can have an ordered list of agenda items. An agenda item has a title, optional speaker name, duration in minutes, and an optional description. The `order` field controls display sequence. Deleting a session cascades to its agenda items.

## `bookings`

The `bookings` table is the most complex entity in the schema, tracking the full lifecycle of a seat reservation from initial hold through payment, potential refund, and venue check-in. Each booking links a user, a session, and a specific seat. A `payment_status` field drives the state machine (`hold`, `paid`, `refunded`, `cancelled`). On payment, the system generates a unique `ticket_id` (formatted as `TT-YYYYMMDD-XXXXXXXX`), a base64-encoded QR code, and records the amount paid based on the seat's type and the session's pricing tiers. Multi-seat purchases share a `booking_group` identifier and a group-level QR code. Cancellations apply a fixed fee of 100 and record both the refund amount and the fee.

| Column | Type | Constraints | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `id` | `Integer` | PK, indexed | Auto-increment primary key |
| `user_id` | `Integer` | FK -> `users.id`, not null | Booking owner |
| `session_id` | `Integer` | FK -> `lecture_sessions.id`, not null | Target session |
| `seat_id` | `Integer` | FK -> `seats.id`, not null | Reserved seat |
| `payment_status` | `String(20)` | Default `"hold"` | One of: `hold`, `paid`, `refunded`, `cancelled` |
| `booking_ref` | `String(20)` | Unique | Auto-generated reference code |
| `ticket_id` | `String(30)` | Unique, nullable | Generated on payment |
| `qr_code_data` | `Text` | Nullable | Base64 QR code of ticket ID |
| `booking_group` | `String(20)` | Nullable, indexed | Group ID for multi-seat bookings |
| `group_qr_data` | `Text` | Nullable | Base64 QR code of group ID |
| `amount_paid` | `Float` | Default `0` | Amount paid |
| `refund_amount` | `Float` | Nullable | Refund after cancellation |
| `cancellation_fee` | `Float` | Nullable | Fee deducted (fixed at 100) |
| `checked_in` | `Boolean` | Default `False` | Whether attendee checked in |
| `checked_in_at` | `DateTime` | Nullable | Check-in timestamp |
| `held_until` | `DateTime` | Nullable | Hold expiry time |
| `booked_at` | `DateTime` | Default `utcnow` | Booking creation timestamp |

**Relationships**: `user` (N:1 User), `session` (N:1 LectureSession), `seat` (N:1 Seat)

## `waitlist`

When a session is sold out, users can join a waitlist. Each entry links a user to the sold-out session and tracks whether the user has been notified. When an admin grants priority access, the entry is updated with a `priority_session_id` (the target session) and a `priority_expires_at` timestamp that gives the user a 24-hour window to book before the general public.

## `testimonials` and `newsletter_subscribers`

The `testimonials` table stores student quotes displayed on the home page, with fields for the student's name, college, quote text, and an active flag for visibility control. The `newsletter_subscribers` table is a simple list of unique email addresses with subscription timestamps.

{{< pagebreak >}}

# API Endpoints Reference

TechTrek is a traditional server-rendered web application -- there are no JSON API endpoints. Every route returns a complete HTML page via Jinja2 templates, and form submissions use `POST` with a 303 redirect on success. The application defines five routers that are registered in order: Auth, Public, Booking, Admin, and Supervisor. Endpoints are protected at four authorization levels: **Public** (no login required), **User** (must be logged in), **Supervisor+** (logged in with `is_supervisor` or `is_admin`), and **Admin** (must have `is_admin=True`).

## Auth Router (`/auth`)

The auth router handles user registration, login, and logout. Registration accepts email, username, password, and optional profile fields; the first user to register is automatically granted admin privileges. Login authenticates by username or email with bcrypt verification and stores `user_id` in the session cookie. Logout clears the session entirely.

| Method | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `GET/POST` | `/auth/login` | Render login form / authenticate and redirect |
| `GET/POST` | `/auth/register` | Render registration form / create account and auto-login |
| `GET` | `/auth/logout` | Clear session and redirect to home |

## Public Router and Booking Router

The public router serves the home page (with up to 6 upcoming sessions and testimonials), the session listing page (with search, sort, and city/college filters), individual session detail pages, and shareable ticket views. The newsletter subscription endpoint accepts an email via `POST`.

The booking router handles the full seat reservation flow for authenticated users. It serves the interactive seat selection page, processes seat holds (5-minute timer), renders the checkout page, confirms payment (generating ticket IDs and QR codes), and displays the confirmation page. Users can also view their booking history, see individual or group booking details, cancel bookings (with a fixed cancellation fee), and join the waitlist for sold-out sessions.

| Router | Method | Path | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Public | `GET` | `/` | Home page |
| Public | `GET` | `/sessions` | Session listing with search and filters |
| Public | `GET` | `/sessions/{id}` | Session detail page |
| Public | `GET` | `/ticket/{ticket_id}` | Shareable single ticket view |
| Public | `GET` | `/tickets/group/{group_id}` | Shareable group ticket view |
| Public | `POST` | `/newsletter` | Subscribe to newsletter |
| Booking | `GET` | `/booking/select/{id}` | Seat selection page |
| Booking | `POST` | `/booking/hold/{id}` | Hold selected seats (5 min) |
| Booking | `GET` | `/booking/checkout/{id}` | Checkout with countdown timer |
| Booking | `POST` | `/booking/pay/{id}` | Confirm payment, generate tickets |
| Booking | `GET` | `/booking/confirmation/{id}` | Confirmation with QR codes |
| Booking | `GET` | `/booking/my` | My Bookings dashboard |
| Booking | `POST` | `/booking/cancel/{id}` | Cancel booking with refund |
| Booking | `POST` | `/booking/waitlist/{id}` | Join session waitlist |

## Admin Router (`/admin`)

The admin router provides a comprehensive management panel accessible to administrators and, for read-only views, supervisors. The dashboard (`GET /admin/`) displays aggregate statistics including total users, bookings, revenue, upcoming sessions, check-ins, and event status breakdowns.

**Resource management** follows a consistent CRUD pattern across six entities. Cities, colleges, speakers, and sessions each provide list, create, edit, and delete endpoints. Cities additionally support an active/inactive toggle. Auditoriums extend the standard CRUD pattern with a visual seat layout designer (`GET/POST /admin/auditoriums/{id}/layout`) that lets administrators paint seat types onto a grid. Session creation and editing include inline agenda item management.

**Booking management** lets supervisors and admins view and filter all bookings by search term, status, or session, and export the full list as a CSV file. Admins can cancel or refund individual bookings. The **check-in page** (`GET/POST /admin/checkin`) supports both QR scanning and manual ticket ID entry, with live statistics for the selected session. The **waitlist page** lets admins grant priority booking access by selecting a sold-out source session and a target session, giving all waitlisted users a 24-hour priority window. The **user management page** lists all registered users and allows admins to toggle admin and supervisor roles.

## Supervisor Router (`/supervisor`)

The supervisor router provides a standalone, simplified check-in interface at `/supervisor/` without the admin sidebar. It supports the same QR scanning and manual ticket verification as the admin check-in page, with immediate visual feedback on ticket validity and live check-in statistics.

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Frontend Architecture

TechTrek deliberately avoids single-page application frameworks. Every page is rendered on the server as complete HTML and delivered to the browser in a single round trip. This approach simplifies development, eliminates client-side routing complexity, and ensures the application works without JavaScript for basic browsing. Interactive features like seat selection, QR scanning, and the layout editor are layered on top through targeted JavaScript modules.

## Templating

The application uses 33 Jinja2 templates organized in an inheritance hierarchy. The root `base.html` provides the shared shell -- navbar, footer, theme toggle, flash messages, starfield background, and global JavaScript utilities. Child templates override named blocks (`title`, `head_extra`, `content`, `scripts`) to inject page-specific content. Admin pages add a second inheritance layer through `admin/base_admin.html`, which extends the base and wraps content in a sidebar navigation layout. Admin-specific templates override the `admin_content` block rather than `content` directly.

## Design System

The entire visual design is contained in a single 2,600-line CSS file (`app/static/css/style.css`) built entirely with CSS custom properties. This makes theming straightforward: switching between dark and light mode requires only overriding a set of CSS variables on the root `<html>` element.

The design uses three font families loaded from Google Fonts: **Orbitron** for headings and hero text, **Exo 2** for body copy and form labels, and **JetBrains Mono** for code, ticket IDs, and booking references.

The default **dark theme** follows a cyberpunk aesthetic with a near-black background (`#010409`), layered dark surfaces for depth, and vibrant accent colors: cyan for primary interactions, amber for prices and VIP elements, violet for user bookings, green for success states, and red for errors. The **light theme** overrides all variables with a Frutiger Aero-inspired palette -- translucent white surfaces, dark text, and adjusted accent tones for readability.

Layout uses a fixed 64px navbar, a 1200px max-width content container, and a consistent set of border-radius values (4px through 20px). The seat map uses a dedicated set of six color variables to distinguish available, taken, selected, VIP, accessible, and user-owned seats.

## Component Library

All UI components are implemented in pure CSS without a JavaScript framework. The system includes a full **button** set with primary, outline, amber, and danger variants in small, standard, and large sizes. **Cards** support hover effects, image headers, and status badges (available, filling up, sold out, priority only). **Forms** provide consistently styled inputs, selects, textareas, and layout helpers. **Modals** implement focus trapping and keyboard dismissal for confirmation dialogs. **Flash messages** auto-dismiss after five seconds with fade-out animations. **Tables** support striped rows and action cells for admin views.

## Interactive JavaScript

Rather than using a client-side framework, TechTrek embeds JavaScript directly in the base template and ships two standalone modules for the most interactive features. This approach keeps the dependency footprint minimal -- the only external library is `html5-qrcode` loaded from a CDN for camera-based QR scanning.

The **base template** includes inline scripts that handle the animated starfield background (dark theme only), navbar scroll effects, mobile hamburger menu, theme persistence via `localStorage`, flash message auto-dismiss, scroll-reveal entrance animations, form submission spinners, a confirmation modal system with focus trapping, a programmatic toast notification API (`window.TechTrek.showToast()`), QR code zoom modals, and the admin sidebar toggle.

The **seat picker** (`seat-picker.js`) powers the booking flow's interactive seat selection page. It receives seat data and pricing tiers as JSON from the server, renders a grid of seat buttons with row labels and visual gaps matching the auditorium layout, and lets users click seats to toggle selection. A live summary panel shows selected seats, per-seat prices, and total cost. The component includes full ARIA accessibility (`role="grid"`, `role="gridcell"`) and responsive viewport scaling.

The **seat designer** (`seat-designer.js`) is the admin-facing auditorium layout editor. It provides paint tools for different seat types (standard, VIP, accessible, aisle, eraser) with click-and-drag interaction. Administrators can fill entire rows or columns, insert or delete rows and columns, create center aisles, adjust stage width, and toggle row/column gaps. On save, the layout is serialized to JSON and submitted via hidden form fields.

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Getting Started

This section covers everything needed to get TechTrek running on a local development machine. The setup process is straightforward: install Python dependencies, ensure PostgreSQL is available, optionally seed the database with sample data, and start the server. No Docker, Node.js, or frontend build step is required.

## Prerequisites

- **Python 3.10+**
- **PostgreSQL** running locally (or update `DATABASE_URL` to point elsewhere)

## Installation

```bash
# Clone the repository
git clone <repo-url>
cd techtrek

# Create and activate a virtual environment
python -m venv .venv
.venv\Scripts\activate       # Windows
# source .venv/bin/activate  # macOS/Linux

# Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

## Database Setup

Ensure PostgreSQL is running and a database named `techtrek` exists:

```sql
CREATE DATABASE techtrek;
```

The application creates all tables automatically on startup. No manual migration step is needed.

## Seed Sample Data

```bash
python seed.py
```

This populates the database with sample cities, colleges, auditoriums, speakers, sessions, testimonials, and user accounts. It only runs if the `users` table is empty.

## Run the Application

```bash
uvicorn app.main:app --reload
```

The app will be available at `http://localhost:8000`. API docs (Swagger UI) are at `http://localhost:8000/api/docs` when `debug=True`.

## Default Accounts

| Username | Password | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `admin` | `admin123` | Admin |
| `alice` | `user123` | Regular user |
| `bob` | `user123` | Regular user |
| `charlie` | `user123` | Regular user |

## Environment Variables

Create a `.env` file in the project root to override any settings. All settings have sensible defaults for local development.

| Variable | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgresql+psycopg2://postgres:root@localhost:5432/techtrek` | PostgreSQL connection string |
| `SECRET_KEY` | `techtrek-dev-secret-change-in-production` | Session cookie signing key (change in production) |
| `DEBUG` | `True` | Enable debug mode and Swagger docs |
| `HOLD_TIMEOUT_MINUTES` | `5` | How long seat holds last before expiring |
| `PRIORITY_WINDOW_HOURS` | `24` | Duration of waitlist priority access window |
| `SMTP_HOST` | (empty) | SMTP server hostname; leave empty to skip emails |
| `SMTP_PORT` | `587` | SMTP server port |
| `SMTP_USER` | (empty) | SMTP login username |
| `SMTP_PASSWORD` | (empty) | SMTP login password |
| `SMTP_FROM_EMAIL` | `noreply@techtrek.in` | Sender email address |
| `SMTP_FROM_NAME` | `TechTrek` | Sender display name |

## Dependencies

```
fastapi
uvicorn[standard]
sqlalchemy
psycopg2-binary
jinja2
python-multipart
bcrypt
itsdangerous
python-dotenv
pydantic-settings
qrcode[pil]
```
